name: Sandia Data Challenge
organization: Department of Statistics & Sandia National Laboratories
highlights: A bio-data competition hosted by the Department of Statistics and Sandia National Laboratories, focused on brainwave analysis and predictive modeling.
Analyzed brainwave data from 40 participants to study the N400 phenomenon and extract features for model training and testing.
Proposed and implemented a Random Forest model as the team’s initial prediction framework.
Coordinated task division and guided team efforts during model development, contributing to efficient collaboration.

name: OVAL International Business Contest
highlights: International business case competition between students from China, Japan, and Korea.
Collaborated in a 3-person team to design a business plan for a personalized diabetes nutrition service.
Conducted market research using TAM / SAM / SOM.
Built financial forecasts in Excel (cash flow, profitability, user growth, etc.).

name: UIUC Datathon — Credit Risk & Spending Forecasting Competition
highlights: A financial data science competition focused on analyzing real-world banking transaction and credit datasets to forecast customer spending, predict credit line increases, and assess associated risks.
Designed and implemented feature engineering pipelines leveraging account history, transaction data, and fraud indicators to enhance model robustness.
Led the modeling effort by constructing risk forecasting frameworks, including XGBoost for credit line increase classification and SARIMA for spending projections.
Coordinated team workflow by drafting the project README, hosting discussions, and ensuring collaborative integration of models and visualizations into a coherent solution.

name: DRW Crypto Forecasting Competition
highlights: A quantitative trading competition on Kaggle hosted by DRW, focused on time-series forecasting of crypto markets.
Context: minute-level historical crypto data; goal was to predict future market price movements.
Utilized train data (525,886 × 786) and test data (538,150 × 786) from the competition.
Led a 5-member team on task allocation, progress tracking, and strategy meetings for timely deliverables.
Designed and implemented predictive models using LSTM and XGBoost.
Spearheaded data pipeline work: API-based data acquisition, visualization, and mutual information–based feature selection.

name: Kamin Atsavasirilert (Capital One) - CMBS Prepayment Modeling Practicum
highlights: Developing a traditional statistical model and a machine learning model to forecast CMBS prepayment behavior.
Engineered loan-level and macroeconomic features, performed model calibration, and evaluated predictive performance using out-of-sample validation; model accuracy ~80%.
Compared statistical and ML approaches to assess robustness, interpretability, and practical applicability in structured finance modeling.

name: Neural Network Approximation of American Option Pricing
organization: MSFE Program, Machine Learning in Finance Lab — Final Project
highlights: Developed neural-network surrogate models to approximate American put option prices under multiple volatility structures.
Generated training data using finite-difference PDE solvers under Black–Scholes and Heston models.
Constructed and analyzed volatility surfaces, including constant volatility, synthetic smile, and market-implied volatility.
Calibrated Heston stochastic volatility and SVI implied volatility surfaces; derived local volatility via the Dupire framework.
Achieved MAE / RMSE of 0.0837 / 0.1120 for Black–Scholes and 0.1453 / 0.1971 for Heston surrogate pricing.

name: LLM-based Financial News Intelligence Pipeline
highlights: Developed a multi-agent financial news analysis pipeline using LangGraph and LLMs for real-time market intelligence.
Integrated FinBERT pre-screening and semantic deduplication to filter and prioritize relevant financial signals.
Designed tiered LLM analysis to generate structured desk-brief reports and alert high-impact events.
Deployed system with FastAPI, Streamlit dashboard, and multi-channel alerting for practical monitoring use.

name: Autonomous ML Research & Optimization System
organization: MSFE Program, High Frequency Trading Course Project
highlights: Built an autonomous research loop that iteratively improves financial ML models using LLM-driven hypothesis generation.
Implemented two-stage evaluation with walk-forward validation and multi-agent judging for robustness and consistency.
Automated experiment tracking, model versioning, and rollback via a Git-based pipeline.
Designed the system for continuous 24/7 model optimization without human intervention.
